name.latinName: Allium sativum var. ophioscorodon (Ophioscorodon group — hardneck garlic; Rocambole subtype)

range.nativeRange: Central Asia (origin likely Kazakhstan/Kyrgyzstan region); cultivated globally for over 7,000 years

usNativeStatus.description: Not native to the US; cultivated throughout the US; not naturalized in the wild

invasive.description: Not invasive; a cultivated annual/biennial that does not establish in the wild

zone.description: 4-9 for hardneck types; Zone 3 with good mulching

description.growthRate: Planted in fall; grows slowly through winter; rapid growth in spring; harvest July
description.timeToMaturity: 9 months from planting to harvest (fall planting to summer harvest); scapes harvestable in June
description.lifespan: Annual crop — replant each fall; save the largest cloves for replanting to improve your local strain over time. OR Perennial - hardneck varieties: leave in the ground and a new bulb will grow from each clove.
description.deciduousEvergreen: Annual — foliage dies back naturally as the bulb matures; this is the harvest signal
description.winterInterest: Green shoots may emerge in mild periods; dormant in deep winter

lightAndWater.sun: Full sun strongly preferred; reduces yield in shade
lightAndWater.water: Medium during active growth; reduce watering as harvest approaches (May onward) — wet conditions at harvest invite fungal rot; good drainage critical for bulb quality

drainagePreference.description: Well-drained essential — garlic bulbs rot in poorly-drained, wet soil; this is the primary challenge in clay-heavy kitchen strips; raise bed, amend with compost, or plant on a slight slope to improve drainage for garlic

claySoilPerformance.description: Poor; garlic bulbs need loose, well-drained soil to develop properly; clay causes compaction around developing bulbs, waterlogging risk that promotes rot, and difficulty at harvest; raised beds are strongly recommended for heavy-soil gardeners wanting quality garlic

soil.texturePreference: Light, well-drained loam preferred; heavy clay requires amendment with compost and sharp sand; loose soil allows bulbs to expand to full size
soil.rootCharacter: True bulb (comprised of multiple cloves); each clove produces one plant; fibrous roots from the basal plate

bloom.bloomTime: June (scapes form in hardneck types); flowers would form July-August if scapes not removed
bloom.bloomDescription: Scapes are curling green flower stalks that emerge from the center of hardneck garlic; cutting scapes diverts energy to bulb development and the scapes themselves are a culinary delicacy. If allowed to develop fully, globular flower heads produce small bulbils rather than seeds.
bloom.fragrance: Strongly aromatic foliage and bulb — the characteristic garlic scent
bloom.flowerSex: Hermaphrodite; mostly sterile in cultivated garlic — reproduces vegetatively
bloom.pollination: Insect-pollinated if flowers develop; primarily vegetative reproduction

yield.edibleParts: Bulb (primary harvest — cloves used in cooking; raw garlic has more potent medicinal compounds than cooked); scapes (the curling stems of hardneck types — one of the garden's best early summer treats; mild garlic flavor, excellent grilled, in pesto, or sautéed); green tops (usable like chives while growing); bulbils (small cloves in the flower head — edible; can be planted)
yield.harvestSeason: Scapes June; bulbs July (when the bottom 3-4 leaves have yellowed); cure bulbs for 3-4 weeks in a dry, airy location before storage
yield.medicinalUses: One of the most extensively studied medicinal plants — allicin (released when garlic is crushed) has documented antibacterial, antifungal, antiviral, and cardiovascular benefits; lowers LDL cholesterol and blood pressure; immune-stimulating; antimicrobial for gut pathogens; arguably the most clinically supported of all herbal medicines; raw garlic (crushed and allowed to sit before consumption) retains the most active allicin
yield.craftOtherYield: Garlic braid (cured softneck garlic; hardneck types have stiffer stems); garlic-infused oil; fermented garlic

cautionsAndHazards.toxicityPeople: Non-toxic in culinary quantities; very large quantities can cause GI irritation; garlic supplements at high doses may interact with blood-thinning medications
cautionsAndHazards.toxicityAnimals: TOXIC to dogs and cats — allium compounds cause hemolytic anemia; even small amounts consumed regularly cause cumulative damage; never feed garlic to dogs or cats; toxic to horses and livestock; this is a significant caution if pets access the garden
cautionsAndHazards.allelopathy: Garlic root exudates suppress fungal pathogens and some soil bacteria — a positive effect as a companion plant; the pest-deterrent and disease-suppressing effects are well-documented

roles: Food (bulb and scapes — high-priority culinary crop)
Medicinal (one of the most important medicinal plants in the garden)
Pest Confuser (companion planting throughout the garden — root exudates deter pests and suppress pathogens)
companionPlants: Roses (garlic at the base of roses is a classic permaculture companion — deters aphids and fungal disease)
brassicas; essentially beneficial throughout the food garden
antagonists: Peas and beans — garlic and other alliums inhibit legume growth; do not interplant with legumes; be aware when planting near Trifolium (clover) ground cover
permaculture.coppiceable: Harvest scapes in June (the curling green stems) — this is the "coppice" equivalent for garlic; removing scapes redirects energy to bulb development; it is both a harvest and a management practice

wildlife.attracts: Minimal direct wildlife value; the strong scent deters many animals

deer.description: Excellent — all Allium species are strongly avoided by deer; garlic's strong scent is a highly effective deer deterrent; planting garlic around the perimeter or within other beds provides some protection to neighboring plants

drought.description: Moderate during active growth; needs moisture when growing vigorously in spring

tolerances.other: Extremely cold hardy when properly mulched; hardneck types are well-adapted to cold winters; mulch with straw after planting in October-November to protect through winter; unmulched bulbs survive Zone 6a but mulching improves spring growth

maintenance.maintenanceLevel: Low to medium — primarily the planting and harvest cycle
maintenance.pruningNotes: Cut scapes in June when they form 1-2 full curls; use immediately or refrigerate for several weeks. Harvest bulbs in July when the bottom 3-4 leaves have turned yellow-brown; do not wait until all leaves die or bulb wrappers will deteriorate. Cure in a dry location with good airflow for 3-4 weeks before storage. Save the largest, best-formed bulbs for replanting to continuously improve your strain. Break up the planting area and replant each fall.
propagationMethods: Clove planting (plant individual cloves point-up, 2-3" deep, in fall; each clove becomes a full bulb by the following July); bulbils (small cloves from the scape head — these take 2-3 years to produce a full-sized bulb but can be used to multiply stock)

cultivarNotes:
Hardneck vs. Softneck by Region: Hardneck garlic (including Rocambole) requires a period of cold vernalization to form proper bulbs — it is the correct choice for Zones 3-6 (cold winters). Softneck garlic (Artichoke and Silverskin types) does not require cold vernalization and is better suited to Zones 7-9 (mild winters) or climates without reliable winter cold; softneck also stores significantly longer (6-12 months vs. Rocambole's 3-6 months). Growers in Zones 5-7 can grow either; growers in Zones 3-5 should grow hardneck exclusively; growers in Zones 8-9 should grow softneck unless they can provide cold stratification.
Rocambole Type: Rocambole types have loose, tan-purple wrappers, rich complex flavor, and easy-to-peel cloves — widely considered the best-flavored garlic type. The tradeoff is shorter storage life (3-6 months) and a requirement for cold winters to bulb properly.
Recommended Rocambole Varieties (Zones 4-7): **'Spanish Roja'** (classic Rocambole, complex flavor, Zone 4-9 — one of the most recommended for flavor); **'German Red'** (reliable, stores reasonably well for a Rocambole); **'Killarney Red'** (high-yielding, good flavor). Source from a reputable garlic supplier for planting stock — grocery store garlic is often treated to inhibit sprouting.

gardenUse.designPlacement: Kitchen-adjacent herb strip — the most practical placement for regular kitchen access; plant a bed of 50-100 cloves each fall for an annual harvest that covers much of the household's garlic needs; drainage is the key challenge in clay soils — amend the garlic bed with compost and avoid low-lying areas; also include garlic cloves in fruit tree guild understories as companion plants
gardenUse.acquisitionStatus: To source (order from garlic suppliers September for fall planting; do not rely on grocery store garlic)

pennsylvaniaHegins.nativeToSite: Not native to PA; cultivated crop
pennsylvaniaHegins.drainage: Hegins red clay: raised beds strongly recommended; in-ground planting with deep compost amendment is a fallback but raised beds produce far superior bulb quality
pennsylvaniaHegins.soil: Raised beds preferred; in-ground with heavy compost amendment is secondary option
pennsylvaniaHegins.cultivarNotes: Rocambole/hardneck garlic is ideal for Zone 6a — the cold winters provide the vernalization needed for proper bulbing; plant in October, harvest July. Recommended varieties for Zone 6a: 'Spanish Roja', 'German Red', 'Killarney Red', or any reputable Rocambole strain sourced from a mid-Atlantic or northeastern garlic supplier; these have been selected for performance in Zone 5-7 conditions.
pennsylvaniaHegins.designPlacement: Raised beds in Section B kitchen garden are the preferred placement — the Hegins red clay makes in-ground garlic difficult; plant in October for Zone 6a timing; harvest July; raised beds allow the drainage and soil looseness garlic requires; Section B's kitchen-adjacent position makes it the logical garlic home
